skill.name: line-limit
skill.description: Enforce practical line-count limits for implementation files, with 200 lines recommended and 300 lines treated as a required modularization threshold.
skill.summary: Check implementation file length and guide modular refactoring when files grow too large.
skill.author: Doyajin174

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many Ruby or shell backtick matches, but review found these are Markdown fences and inline code examples, not executable Ruby or shell code. The weak cryptography blocker at SKILL.md line 3 is also a false positive; no cryptographic code or algorithm use was found. The skill does request Bash for local line counting, so it carries a low external-command risk but shows no malicious intent, network use, secret access, or prompt injection.

low_findings.title: Bash Permission Used for Local Line Counting
low_findings.description: The skill declares Bash access and documents a local line-count command. This is consistent with its purpose, but Bash is a broad execution capability and should remain limited to trusted local inspection commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The declared Bash tool and documented wc command are direct evidence of external command capability. The command is local and narrowly related to line counting, so the risk is low.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backtick Command Matches Dismissed
low_findings.description: The static Ruby or shell backtick findings are Markdown inline-code markers and fenced examples for file extensions, pseudo output, and refactoring layouts. No executable Ruby backtick expression or command-substitution behavior was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged regions are Markdown tables and code fences visible in the skill documentation. They are not source files that execute commands.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Finding Dismissed
low_findings.description: The static high-risk blocker at SKILL.md line 3 does not correspond to cryptographic code. The line is a natural-language description of 200 and 300 line-count limits, with no hashing, encryption, or deprecated algorithm use.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact line contains only the skill description and numeric line thresholds. No cryptography API, algorithm name, or security-sensitive implementation appears there.

content.value_statement: Large implementation files make reviews, changes, and debugging harder. This skill checks file length and guides modular refactoring before code becomes difficult to maintain.
actual_capabilities: Checks implementation files against 200-line and 300-line thresholds.
Separates code implementation files from documentation, fixtures, generated files, and configuration files.
Suggests modularization plans for oversized files.
Provides refactoring patterns for React, TypeScript, Python, Go, and general utility code.
Encourages user approval before splitting files during refactoring.
limitations: It does not enforce limits for documentation, notebooks, configuration files, generated files, or style sheets.
It relies on local line counts and code review judgment.
It does not automatically prove that a split improves design.
It may need project-specific exceptions for generated or framework-managed code.

use_cases.title: Review Oversized Source Files
use_cases.description: Identify implementation files above the recommended or maximum line thresholds and receive a focused split plan.

use_cases.title: Keep New Features Modular
use_cases.description: Monitor file size while creating new components, services, or helpers so the design stays easier to review.

use_cases.title: Plan Refactoring Work
use_cases.description: Break a large file into logical modules such as components, hooks, types, constants, helpers, or services.

prompt_templates.title: Check One File
prompt_templates.prompt: Check the line count for this implementation file and tell me whether it is OK, warning, or violation.
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of a single source file

prompt_templates.title: Review a Feature Change
prompt_templates.prompt: Review the changed implementation files for line-limit violations and list any files that should be split.
prompt_templates.scenario: Code review before merging a feature

prompt_templates.title: Create a Split Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: This file is over 300 lines. Analyze its sections and propose a modularization plan before editing.
prompt_templates.scenario: Planning a refactor for a large file

prompt_templates.title: Refactor With Boundaries
prompt_templates.prompt: Refactor this oversized implementation file into cohesive modules, keep each new file near 200 lines, and preserve behavior.

output_examples.output: The file is 342 lines, so it violates the 300-line maximum. Suggested split: endpoint functions, shared types, and helper utilities.

output_examples.input: This dashboard component is becoming large. What should I extract?
output_examples.output: Extract the header, statistics panel, chart component, data hook, types, and constants into separate cohesive files.

output_examples.input: Check whether my new utility file needs refactoring.
output_examples.output: The file is within the recommended range. No split is required, but keep unrelated helpers grouped by responsibility.

best_practices: Apply the limit to implementation files, not documentation or generated files.
Split by responsibility instead of splitting only to reduce line count.
Confirm build, tests, imports, and exports after moving code.
anti_patterns: Do not split a cohesive module into many one-function files.
Do not create circular dependencies while extracting code.
Do not refactor large files without first identifying logical sections.

faq.question: What line limits does this skill use?
faq.answer: It treats 200 lines as the recommended target and 300 lines as the maximum for implementation files.

faq.question: Does it apply to Markdown or configuration files?
faq.answer: No. The skill excludes documentation, configuration files, generated files, notebooks, fixtures, declarations, and style sheets.

faq.question: Can it refactor files automatically?
faq.answer: It can guide and perform refactoring when the active tool supports editing, but it should present a plan first.

faq.question: Which languages are covered?
faq.answer: The skill covers common implementation files such as JavaScript, TypeScript, Python, Go, Rust, Java, Kotlin, C, and C++.

faq.question: Will every file over 200 lines be split?
faq.answer: No. Files from 201 to 300 lines receive a warning, while files over 300 lines require modularization review.

faq.question: How does it decide where to split code?
faq.answer: It looks for responsibilities such as components, hooks, types, constants, helpers, services, and language-specific modules.
